item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image originates from the public domain. As such, it is compliant with CC 3.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this has a lot of potential, but I would reclassify it as euclid. Even someone else possessing a colony of it just gives them access to chlorine triflouride, which I know what that stuff is and how dangerous it is but it's not like…Fungal spores that when inhaled by mammals makes them enter an uncontrolled fission reaction or anything. Massively damaging to LOCAL ecology but definitely not an end of the world scenario, even normal human populations could eradicate identify and control it. Plus, not sure if it even falls under foundation purview, seems non anomalous.

If you want to give this a Keter classification I would think of adding some anomalous method of propagation that makes it truly terrifying. But regardless I would love to see some case files tracking this plant, or experiment logs or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd delete it but I can't.

Only mods and admins can delete posts, and we only do so when they contain severe rule-breaking material. You're fine here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this, actually, especially the very human "But you can eat it!" addendum. Shadowy corporations get very same-y after a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edit: I'm so sorry, this was meant to be a new post but I must have clicked reply by mistake. I'd delete it but I can't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe you should just copy the "Hazards" section in Cl2F6 wikipedia's page, label that as an scp and call it a day. That thing seriously gives me the creeps. Having said that, I kind of like what you did here, even if it is, indeed, a little bland.

Upvoted

Actually, no. I can't, in good conscience, upvote this article with that (now) meaningless strikethrough in the Containment instructions, and after the changes, the title seems ironic.

Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a few problems with this SCP:

The struck out portion of the description is ripped straight off of the Wikipedia page for chlorine trifluoride, first paragraph, last sentence.

The article probably benefits from having any reference to the prior use of this material removed completely. There is no further mention of this practice in the article, so it really doesn't do anything for me.

Why is this Keter if it is easily identifiable and easily contained in the wild? The compound itself is scary, sure, but non-anomalous. Just how pervasive is this species?

Why is this disposed of in a "burn chamber" when it can't be combusted via traditional means? Why call it a burn chamber at all?

It feels like this SCP has a lot of disjointed elements, and no unifying idea to tie them all together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is better written than it was before, but it's completely dull and generic. Downvote remains.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seems to bring nothing new to the "Plant scp" table. It's like the most generic keter plant thinkable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hot damn this got neutered. I mean, wow. This used to be about a plant that was harvested by a corrupt corporation for a specific, very dangerous chemical for shadowy purposes. Now, it's just… it's got no bite to it. It's like a pit bull that everyone liked but he bit the kid down the street once and now he's got all his teeth removed as a substitute for euthanasia.

This is utterly bland now. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eeeeeeesh. ClF3 is nasty stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Removed the location reference, streamlined the article, condensed the interesting bits; general improvements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except that it doesn't—ClF3, another 'word' for Cl2F6/chlorine trifloride is a real-life material, despite what the SCP claims.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shit, a chlorine trifloride flower! I totally think that counts as Keter. That shit burns sand, makes water explode, is poisonous as hell and in general makes anyone sane run screaming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what you're getting at with the car analogy, but the mechanic in me is screaming at me for not saying something.

Some repairs to automobile motors require the car to be running, such as testing vaccuum lines or balancing/syncing a carburetor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, it's harder science than you usually find. I really enjoy when someone pulls off a mimic plant well. There have been quite a few entries that haven't been "up to snuff" to put it nicely. So well done!

I do have a slight gripe, though. If I'm misreading this let me know. It looks like you're implying that the first colony's location was Chernobyl, and you put an "R" as the first letter of the second bit. Chernobyl is in Ukraine, not Russia.

And as a public service announcement, I would like to inform the populace that the Chernobyl Disaster, while making a good story for creepy stuff like this, was a tradgedy, both in the people who were killed or irradiated by it, and by the blow to nuclear power's PR. Chernobyl isn't something that should ever happen. It came about because of criminally lax safety measures. What they did was induce a casualty situation in the reactor to try and see how long it would take them to fix it. ON A CRITICAL REACTOR. That's like trying to repair a car motor while it's still running. Only it explodes and kills everyone in the surrounding town.

Sorry for that. I'm a terrible person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't find this very interesting, unfortunately, especially considering the sheer amount of text in the article. I don't think it's bad enough to downvote, but hoping the author plans to keep working on it…

EDIT: No update, still not-incredibly nteresting wall of text, therefore, downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They do, which clears out competing plants, kills any not-close-enough-to-suffocate wildlife and in general is great for the plant's expansion. They're also not perfect mimics, just good ones- a botanist, even an amateur one, could realize something was off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it imitates other plants perfectly and secretes a volatile chemical that reacts with water? Shouldn't these things be exploding en masse every time it rains?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it really would help illustrate what this SCP means, if it was well done. Go for it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is good, but the compound needs to be more than just Cl2F6. A variant or evolution of P680 containing an Antimony- or Bismuth-Cl2F6 halorganic metal complex would make more sense chemically and be more believable from a "holy sh!t" standpoint.

Example

The flowers, roots and vines of SCP-1621 secrete a compound in place of sap or nectar which, following the initial containment of a colony in C████████, R████, was identified to be a variant of P680 pigment containing dichlorine hexafluoride (Cl2F6) in a bismuth organometallic complex. The biomolecule vaporizes in the open at temperatures higher than 13 degrees Celsius, is colorless as a gas and smells sweet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been thinking about putting together an incident report to describe why the plant is so dangerous. Would that help to consolidate the creepiness and fear factor?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno… it feels like it's piggybacking too much off of a pre-existing frightening thing for me to get much enjoyment out of it. That is to say, there's an aspect of it that makes it feel like an anomaly, and there's an aspect of it that scares me, but they feel too separate from each other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Fruit-bearing specimens do not contain CL2F6 and, in fact, if they can be harvested safely, have proven safe to eat and a close approximation of the fruit mimicked."

I find this part creepy. The fruit sound suspiciously like a lure.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this one when it was posted asking for feedback. I still like it. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I had just read to the bottom of the thing, I wouldn't have been wondering why I found all these references to ClF3 but not Cl2F6. Then I found out what just ClF3 could be, then I went back and finished reading your sheet. Holy crap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're right-the phrasing of the last paragraph was so strange that it confused me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cl2F6 is serious business.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but why is it Keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation isn't selling it. They're using it rather than purchasing it, which leaves a paper trail. The point is that the chemical on the market proves that someone ELSE has a colony, which is a threat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that the containment protocol is too long, but otherwise I think it is well written. However, the Foundation should not be selling the chemical. Other chemical manufacturers will try to reproduce it, and fail. This could bring too much attention to bear on the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me, both the containment procedure and the description are too long. Additionally, it rubs me the wrong way that the Foundation would harness a Keter level SCP for the production of a completely non-anomalous chemical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't dislike this, but the 'and also someone is selling it' at the end doesn't seem to fit with the rest of the article.
